data.title: Why The Market Is Wrong On Nvidia: Initiating The Stock With A Buy Under $100
data.description:
I’m initiating Wall Street’s darling Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) with a buy. If you bought at market open, you got the stock at $89, and that’s a steal, so congrats. While a market crash can prove scary to some, I’m buying the panic. And with a company like Nvidia, a buy case isn’t really that complicated to make, but Nvidia under $100 makes an even easier case.

Nvidia under a $100 is a rare occurrence, and if we’re taking the split into account, the last time the stock was trading below a $100 was back in August last year.

The tariff situation

I’m not too worried about Nvidia in regards to tariffs in the short term, and I share CEO Jensen Huang’s sentiment on that. The company is likely exempt from the latest tariff announcements under the USMCA. According to trade data, Nvidia’s U.S. AI server shipments come in largely from Mexico (roughly 60%) and another 30% from Taiwan. So the dilemma going forward is: will Nvidia be able to maintain its GPU pricing power?

Following President Trump’s tariff announcement last week, Nvidia and TSMC (TSM) both fell over 5%, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) down 4%, and Broadcom (AVGO) down another 7%, while Nvidia’s key memory chip supplier Micron Technology (MU) was down 7%. Trump’s 54% total tariffs on China and 32% reciprocal tariffs on Taiwan sent the market into a shock. TSMC, which produces chips for Nvidia, AMD, and Intel (INTC) are at the heart of it all; for some context, the U.S. imported around $19 billion in servers equipped with Nvidia GPUs from Taiwan and $34 billion from China in 2024. Trump thinks the tariffs should bring manufacturing back to the U.S., as he points at TSMC’s $100 billion investment in Arizona, which started way back under the Biden Administration.

Still, the uncertainty around the tariff situation isn’t to be ignored. With the U.S. threatening an additional 50% tariff on China starting Wednesday unless China takes back its 34% reciprocal tariff on the U.S., tariffs on China could total a staggering 104%.

According to the company’s 10-K and 10-Q, revenue from sales to customers outside the U.S. decreased from 69% in the fiscal year 2023 to 56% in the fiscal year 2024 and 53% in the fiscal year 2025 due to “higher U.S.-based Compute & Networking segment demand.” The U.S. accounted for around 46.9% in revenue for FY25, up from around 44.2% in FY24 and around 30.7% in FY23. So while this is a more balanced exposure, the company still makes a significant amount of its sales from the rest of the world and that means if other nations like Singapore or Taiwan place tariffs on the U.S. in retaliation to Trump’s Liberation Day, then Nvidia could be looking at trouble.

The U.S. now makes nearly half of Nvidia’s revenue, as seen below. Exposure to Singapore and Taiwan is still relevant. but the latter is shrinking to around 16% in FY25, it’s down from 22% in FY24 and 25% in FY23.

I think we’re likely to see this from countries other than China because what we’ve seen so far indicates that Trump wants to cut deals and negotiate, and it looks like that’s a more likely scenario, or an eventual scenario, over a back and forth trade war between the U.S. and everyone else.

When it comes to China, I like that Nvidia is reducing its exposure to China, with revenue from China down from 21.5% in FY23 to around 13.1% in FY25, cushioning the company against U.S. chip restrictions. All in all, I think Nvidia is better positioned to maneuver the effects of the tariffs and remain a leader in the space in the long term.

Valuation and price movement:

The stock was down over 7.9% to $86 per share at market open on Monday, but rebounded rather quickly back to $94, and closed over 3.5% higher at $97.6. There is still a buy-on-the-dip opportunity here, with the stock down over 13.3% over the past 5 days, down another 16% on the one-month chart, and a staggering 31% year to date. I don’t see much downside ahead.

The stock is currently trading under its EMA21 at $111.2, EMA50 at $118.9, and EMA200 at $119.5, as seen below.

RSI was at around 32, nearing oversold territory at the beginning of the trading day on Monday, but has since recovered slightly and currently stands at 33, signaling bullish momentum.

The valuation metrics also look attractive at current levels in comparison to its peers and its past. Nvidia’s forward P/E at 20.8, slightly higher than AMD's at 18.3, and significantly lower than Intel's at 40. Nvidia’s forward PEG ratio is currently at 0.59, below AMD at 0.63, and the sector median at 1.29.

Bullish Investment Thesis:

Nvidia is an AI leader, and no one has come close, with competitors like Advanced Micro and Intel struggling to take market share. I’m optimistic about Nvidia for the long term and think the company is ahead of competitors and the market alike. I see some volatility in the short term, with all the tariff uncertainty, but I think this is a good time to start buying in bunches.

The company led the data center GPU market and held a 92% market share, a step closer to total dominance. Through 2024, the company’s quarterly revenue was on the rise, with a record annual revenue of over $115 billion in 2024, up a staggering 142% year over year. Nvidia’s strategy is to be the all-in-one supplier of AI data centers, and continuous software innovations (and all the time spent working on improving CUDA, which is currently the company’s biggest moat) have helped maintain the crown, not to mention the most recent Blackwell-architecture GPUs which have become the new standard for data center GPUs.

Going forward, I’m not only optimistic about Nvidia for being a leader in the GPU market, but also other markets it could potentially be a leader in like the data center networking market and the PC client market. On the former, the question now is: who can deliver switches with higher bandwidth and lower latency?

Nvidia is now trying to create a new standard by “integrating silicon photonics directly into switches, Nvidia is shattering the old limitations of hyperscale and enterprise networks and opening the gate to million-GPU AI factories,” according to the company’s CEO Jensen Huang. At GTC 2025, Nvidia introduced its Spectrum-X Photonics (to arrive in 2026) and Quantum-X Photonics (to be released in 2025) networking switch platforms, which up data transfer speed to 1.6 Tb/s per port (twice the maximum of current top-tier copper Ethernet solutions, and 400 Tb/s in the aggregate), which allows millions of GPUs to operate together rather seamlessly. According to the company, the new switches offer higher bandwidth and lower power loss. In comparison to Broadcom’s open networking environment, which allows data centers to integrate components from various vendors, Nvidia is an advocate for a vertically integrated solution, combining both its hardware and software for optimized performance.

The data center networking market is expected to grow from $37.6 billion in 2024 to $64.2 billion by 2029, at a CAGR of 11.3% with AI and ML integrations projected to boost market demand.

Nvidia dominated the GPU market, and they have the client base, so who’s to say they can’t do it again? I remain optimistic about Nvidia long-term and think the stock's current price is an attractive entry point for investors.

data.title: Utilities Sector Update - Competitive Growth Along With Above-Average Price Stability And Attractive Dividend Yields
data.description:
Our last blog discussed safe harbors amidst the current storm of market volatility. The idea presented was to investigate the strong buy stocks with the lowest betas. This week, we home in on a sector that is typically low volatility and low growth. Currently, we find fairly competitive earnings growth in this sector. We look in detail and compare 6 Utilities stocks, and 7 Utility Sector ETFs in this post.

The Utilities industry in the United States is a cornerstone of economic stability and growth, providing essential services such as electricity, gas, and water to millions of consumers. This report focuses on six notable US domiciled stocks in the Utilities sector, identified by their ticker symbols: XEL, D, NEE, UGI, AEP, and AWK. All are rated as 5 (Strong Buy) by ValuEngine’s predictive model.

I include a brief synopsis of the predictive model’s methodology for the uninitiated. The model uses proprietary algorithms that incorporate financial metrics such as earnings, forward estimates, interest rate forecasts, revenue growth, profit margins, and return on equity to assess stock performance. The methodology emphasizes fundamental analysis, examining key financial statements, industry trends, and company-specific factors to determine intrinsic value. Stocks are compared within their industry peers. ValuEngine also factors in risk assessment, considers market volatility, economic conditions, and company-specific risks to provide a comprehensive evaluation. This allows us to evaluate relative performance and identify potential investment opportunities. Those relative opportunities are characterized by our ratings which range from 1 (Strong Sell) to 5 (Strong Buy).

We take a bottom-up approach to sector evaluation to create our Sector Report. Besides low beta and high dividend yield, another reason the Utilities Sector was chosen to be featured today is that it is currently the third highest sector in terms of current investment opportunity with a rating of 3.5 out of a possible 5. I note that historically the utilities sector is for income investors more than for investors looking for price appreciation. Because of this, the sector has commonly been in the bottom half of forecast price gains. There are a number of fundamental underpinnings of the industry which explains the unusual opportunities to derive income, superior return/risk ratios and top-quartile price gains from the sector.

The Utilities industry sector is undergoing a transformative phase characterized by several key trends:

  • A significant shift towards renewable energy sources such as wind, solar, and hydroelectric power is reshaping the Utilities sector. This transition is driven by both regulatory mandates and consumer demand for cleaner energy.
  • The adoption of smart grid technology, enhanced battery storage, and energy management systems is optimizing efficiency and reliability in energy distribution.
  • Stringent environmental regulations and policies are compelling utilities to reduce carbon emissions and invest in sustainable infrastructure.
  • There is a considerable focus on upgrading aging infrastructure to enhance resilience against natural disasters and improve service reliability.

The six Strong Buy 5-rated companies under consideration, XEL, D, NEE, UGI, AEP, and AWK, are well-positioned to capitalize on these trends. In fact, excepting AWK, the rest can be thought of as energy technology companies.

Xcel Energy (XEL) is a leader in the renewable energy transition, with substantial investments in wind and solar power. The company’s commitment to reducing carbon emissions and its innovative solutions make it a key player in the future of energy.

Dominion Energy (D) is at the forefront of infrastructure modernization, with significant projects aimed at enhancing grid reliability and expanding renewable energy capacity. The company’s strategic initiatives align technological trends.

NextEra Energy (NEE) is renowned for its aggressive expansion into renewable energy, particularly wind and solar power. Its extensive portfolio of clean energy projects positions it as a frontrunner in the Utilities sector’s transformation.

UGI Corporation (UGI) focuses on natural gas distribution and energy services, with an emphasis on sustainability and energy efficiency. The company’s diverse operations and commitment to innovation provide a solid foundation for growth.

American Electric Power (AEP) is heavily invested in upgrading its transmission infrastructure and expanding its renewable energy footprint. AEP’s initiatives are geared towards enhancing grid resilience and meeting evolving energy needs.

American Water Works (AWK) is the largest publicly traded water and wastewater utility company in the US. Its focus on infrastructure investment and regulatory compliance ensures the provision of high-quality water services.

Ticker Company Name Utility Type Market Cap ($Bil) VE Rating One Year Forecast Forecast Rank Valuation Ranking Current P/E Forward P/E PEG Earnings Growth Growth Ranking Beta Div. Yield YTD Gain/Loss Last 12 mo. Gain 5-Yr. Price Chg.

(UGI) UGI Corp Gas distribution 7.1 5 14.3% 96 33 10.6 10.7 NMF* -0.5% 22 1.20 4.5% 17.6% 39.2% -1.1%

(SPLG) SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF Market benchmark 52,124.0 4 4.90% 72 38 22.3 20.1 1.6 8.7% N/A 1.00 1.4% -2.7% 8.4% 13.9%

The last three rows, analyzed in the aggregate, bolsters the investment thesis that the past 12 months has provided unusual opportunities for price appreciation for utilities. All six utilities standard-bearers have outgained the S&P 500 ETF during the past 12 months. Only NextEra, the only one of the six to have a negative return thus far in 2025, has underperformed on a Year-to-Date basis. It is not surprising at all that all six stocks have dividend yields considerably higher than SPLG. Dominion Energy’s 5.0% dividend yield is the highest of the group. The returns forecast by the ValuEngine model follows the same pattern as what has been realized during the past 12 months. The projected utility stock returns range from 14.1% to 16.7% as compared with just 4.9% expected from the S&P 500 ETF. Although all six utility stocks are rated strong buys, Xcel Energy (XEL) and Dominion Energy (D) are the two most attractive now. Another plus for D is having an earnings growth rate of 23.6%, the highest in the study.

A similar picture is painted when moving on to ETFs. Given the unusual opportunities to invest for growth as well as income in the Utilities Sector and six of its largest stocks, it may not be as surprising as it would have been normally to find that the three largest Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs) in the Utilities sector also are rated 5 (Strong Buy). As most regular readers of this blog probably know, I was an early pioneer of ETFs and generally recommend most investors that don’t consider themselves traders buy and hold ETFs in lieu of individual stocks, ETFs offer diversified exposure to the industry with very low expense ratios, Accordingly, they have become the tools most commonly used to implement sector rotation strategies in attempts to take advantage of cyclical returns and risk-taking changes in the markets. The six largest Utilities sector ETFs are:

Utility Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLU) is the largest and most popular Utilities sector ETFs, with substantial assets under management. It tracks the performance of the Utilities Select Sector Index, a market-cap-weighted index comprising a broad range of utility companies.

Vanguard Utilities ETF (VPU) follows the performance of the MSCI US Investable Market Utilities 25/50 Index, a larger and more diverse utilities index, but is still market-cap weighted

Fidelity MSCI Utilities Index ETF (FUTY) tracks the performance of the Dow Jones U.S. Utilities Index, providing broad exposure to the Utilities sector. Its holdings include a mix of electricity, gas, and water utilities. FUTY has the lowest expense ratio of the six ETFs at 0.08% (8 basis points, 1 basis point lower than XLU and VP.

iShares Utilities ETF (IDU) tracks the performance of the Dow Jones U.S. Utilities Index, providing broad exposure to the Utilities sector. Its holdings include a mix of electricity, gas, and water utilities. Its expense ratio is at least 30 points higher than that of the above three ETFs without attempting to find excess alpha or other value added.

First Trust Utilities AlphaDEX® Fund (FXU) seeks investment results that correspond generally to the price and yield, before fees and expenses, of an equity index called the StrataQuant® Utilities Index. This proprietary strategy index uses fundamental data relating to earnings strength and valuation to select and weigh the portfolio constituents. It has the highest expense ratio of the six ETFs at a whopping 0.64% (64 basis points). Since it is value-added, the implicit aspiration is that FXU will deliver better performance that more than pays for the fee.

Virtus Reaves Utilities ETF (UTES) aims to provide total return through a combination of capital appreciation and income, primarily by investing in equity securities of companies in the utility sector, and is actively managed, not seeking to replicate a specific index. Once again, SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG) is used for comparisons.

Symbol (XLU) (VPU) (FUTY) (IDU) (FXU) (UTES) SPLG
Name Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund Vanguard Utilities ETF Fidelity MSCI Utilities Index ETF iShares U.S. Utilities ETF First Trust Utilities AlphaDEX Fund Virtus Reaves Utilities ETF SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF

ValuEngine’s ratings are consistent as 5 (Strong Buy) on all the utilities ETFs. This is not surprising since NEE, D and AEP are held by all but one of these ETFs. So, how should an investor distinguish between them?

Clearly, when most investors, traders, managers and pundits discuss US utilities ETFs in a broad sense, XLU (the Utilities Select Sector SPDR) is the first one that comes to mind. It is more than twice as large as all the other ETFs in the study put together. It was also the first one. Its trading volume dwarfs the others put together considerably, closer to ten times all the others put together. Obviously, the vast majority of sector-timing trades using Utility Sector ETFs uses XLU. When it comes to net Fund Flows year-to-date, XLU also dominates this category.

There are some investors, particularly some pension funds, which do prefer a broader universe for investing in utilities than restricting investment to only those utility stocks that made it into the S&P 500 Index. Vanguard’s VPU and Fidelity’s FUTY were both created to serve that purpose, so similar that they use practically identical indexes. FUTY has attempted to distinguish itself on price with an expense ratio that is one basis point lower than those of XLU and VPU. Its negative fund flows year-to-date would tend to indicate that distinction has not been entirely successful in attracting assets.

Since the first four ETFs are all attempting to capture and emulate utility sector performance without attempting superior performance, it makes sense that their returns and investment ratios are almost identical. The question becomes whether the extra expense-ratio percentages paid to quantitatively managed FXU and UTES have been rewarded with disproportionately superior performance.

As a quant, I am somewhat surprised to report that the answer is yes. FXU had superior performance to XLU and the other three in every period, outperforming by higher percentages than the differentials in their expense ratios. Moreover, actively managed UTES, managed by utility specialist Reeves as part of the Virtus Investments family, outperformed by an even greater amount during the past 1-, 3- and 5-year periods. It also seems to be a relative bargain to receive the benefits of its fully active management for 15 basis points less than the algorithmic management used for FXU. There is an asterisk that must go with this statement that UTES is very clear about in its materials. The active management team is focused on providing capital appreciation, not maximizing dividend income. Therefore, UTES is not the best choice for income-focused investors. Investors who choose ETFs for diversification may be more comfortable with VPU or FUTY because UTES has just 23 different holdings.

The bottom line is that with ratings of 5 (Strong Buy), all six ETFs are worthy of consideration. As long as the overall Utilities industry ETFs remain positive, the rest is up to individual preference and due diligence. Driven by the growing demand for sustainable energy and infrastructure modernization and as utilities continue to adapt to regulatory changes and technological advancements, these ETFs are expected to benefit from the sector’s long-term growth prospects. Moreover, this area seems as if it could be one of the relatively safer areas of an economy where tariffs and trade wars are front and center. Having the mandate to adjust prices with input costs is very helpful in such an environment. Not being dependent upon export revenues also helps during such turbulence.

The Utilities industry is at a pivotal juncture, with significant opportunities for growth and innovation. The six highlighted stocks - XEL, D, NEE, UGI, AEP, and AWK - are well-positioned to thrive in this evolving landscape. Using the ValuEngine.com‘s methodology provides valuable insights into their potential performance. Additionally, the largest Utilities industry ETFs offer diversified exposure and are poised to benefit from the sector’s positive outlook for 2025. As the industry continues to evolve, these investments present compelling opportunities for investors seeking stability and growth.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: LifeMD Is Back To Life
data.description:
LifeMD (NASDAQ:LFMD) went through a rough patch with WorkSimpli (its cash cow) stagnating and its weight management segment upstaged by cheaper alternatives (most notably that of HIMS, which is why we switched to the latter).

However, with the end of GLP-1 compounds in sight, LifeMD's competitiveness in weight management might be restored especially as they have put the infrastructure in place to deal with insurance coverage for the expensive brand-name GLP-1 meds.

Q4 results were also much better than the market feared so the shares are rebounding in a very difficult market.

Weight Management

LifeMD used to be at a disadvantage to HIMS with its much bigger membership and its own cheap compounded weight management products.

However, the end of the scarcity of semaglutide (Ozempic and Wegovy) necessitates the cessation of compounding commercially available doses by state-licensed pharmacies and outsourcing facilities by April and May 2025 (except personalized compounds with clinically justified modifications).

As a result, the balance is now shifting back somewhat as the market shifts towards branded products, and LifeMD is at an advantage as it has set up a payer infrastructure for both private insurance companies as well as Medicare.

The company continues offering patients compounded GLP-1s until the FDA's enforcement dates in April and May but will take a considerable write-down on their compounds (almost half of these).

Despite the FDA's decision regarding the end of some GLP-1 brand shortages, the company remains well-positioned in the weight management market (Q4CC):

Importantly, over 85% of our weight management revenue is related to the delivery of care. Helping patients use private insurance to access weight loss medications has been a fundamental part of our service offering since it launched in 2023. While helping patients access, branded GLP-1 therapies was previously difficult due to supply shortages and lower levels of payer coverage. We are seeing several encouraging trends that causes to be very optimistic.

For starters, management is optimistic about expanded insurance coverage, including Medicare, for branded GLP-1 therapies and has prepared infrastructure to facilitate its members getting reimbursement or prior authorization (Q4CC):

Around 10% of patients are receiving coverage for obesity off the bat without us filing a prior authorization. Approximately 60% of patients that have private insurance are required to -- are getting a response that requires them to file a prior authorization. And as we said on the call about 60% of, about 60% of the prior authorizations that we're filing right now for Zepbound for patients that are obese, meaning they have a 30 BMI or higher, are getting really affordable coverage for the drug.

Reaching critical mass is needed to drive conversion rates, they now have private coverage in 20 states and will have national coverage in the latter part of the year.

While current Medicare Part D coverage for GLP-1 drugs requires certain comorbidities, management is optimistic that Medicare will begin covering these medications for obesity and is positioning itself to capitalize on this potential expansion.

The company entered a relationship with a third-party pharmacy used by LillyDirect to ship cash-pay vials of Zepbound directly to patients, enhancing the experience for uninsured patients with LifeMD makes money on the virtual care provided, not the pharmacy transaction in this case.

The company also offers affordable standalone and combination generic weight loss medications, including a triple therapy, a $99/M oral weight loss treatment plan combining metformin, bupropion, and topiramate for people who don't qualify for GLP-1 meds.

These non-GLP-1 weight loss options are currently a small part of the business but are expected to grow if GLP-1 access becomes more difficult.

In addition, the fragmented market with thousands of small players, many of which might not be able to adapt to the changing market with more available GLP-1 meds and compounds prohibited, offers an opportunity for LifeMD.

Insurance coverage

LifeMD is actively working to build insurance coverage for its members through several key efforts, encompassing both private and government payers. Management believes this will be a substantial growth driver for their weight management and broader virtual primary care offerings.

  • The company is actively enrolling with private payers and is currently enrolled in 20 states. This allows patients with insurance in these states to potentially use their coverage for LifeMD's services.
  • The company will launch its fee-for-service Medicare offerings this month, having worked on the compliance and operational infrastructure for this launch for the past two years, aiming to become the easiest and most affordable way for Medicare beneficiaries to access comprehensive obesity care and GLP-1 medications.
  • Management anticipates having approximately 150M lives under coverage by the end of 2025 through its private and government programs. This signifies a significant expansion in their potential insured patient base.
  • The company has been investing in its electronic benefit verification and prior authorization infrastructure to smooth the process of insurance application and reduce the chance of refusal, resulting in an increase in prior authorization rates for GLP-1 meds (like an 18% for Zepbound in Q4, the approval rate is now almost 60%).
  • The strategy includes offering comprehensive virtual primary care, metabolic health, behavioral health, and prescription services, which become more accessible with insurance coverage. The launch of the LifeMD plus membership program is also intended to work with insurance coverage for services like 24/7 care and lab work. A temporary extension of Medicare telehealth coverage is in place.

New products

  • Entry into the behavioral health space is planned for Q2, initially offering async-first treatment for anxiety and depression, with the potential for talk therapy partnerships. This huge market seems very well suited for telehealth solutions.
  • LifeMD Plus, an enhanced virtual care membership program will launch in Q2/25, priced at $19 per month, offering benefits like 24/7 care, on-demand prescriptions, lab work access, and an OTC wellness marketplace.
  • A dedicated women's health specialty offering is expected in H2/25.
  • Personalization; non-sterile compounding, like HIMS
  • Their own pharmacy (reduced GM a couple of points, one-off)
  • HRT for men and women providing quality care, on the men's side Q4CC: "that means that business could be just as big as our ED business or our entire Rex MD business today." There are only a few million dollars in the FY25 guidance for male HRT.
  • HRT for women (Q4CC): "75% of the patients on our virtual primary care and weight loss programs today are women. It's the perfect cross-care program."

Nationwide Pharmacy Launch

LifeMD launched its own pharmacy, which is now licensed in 47 states and shipping approximately 20K orders per month.

  • The pharmacy is initially focused on prescription shipments for the lifestyle healthcare business.
  • Non-sterile compounding capabilities are expected to be operational within the next four to six months, allowing for the expansion of personalized and affordable therapies in areas like HRT, sexual health, and dermatology.
  • The pharmacy is projected to realize $5M of annual economic benefits by the end of 2026.

Finances

  • Growth is still high and recovered well from the slowdown in FY22 with revenues growing 43% to $64.3M in Q4 and core telehealth revenues up 60%. Here is a split (10K):

  • Subscriber growth is also still significant, up 27% to 275K subscribers.

  • Gross margin dipped to 85%, but this was primarily due to one-time costs related to onboarding a new pharmacy. Gross margins are expected to return to normal levels in FY25.

  • OpEx is increasing fairly rapidly as well but GAAP operating margin turned positive and Telehealth adjusted EBITDA was up 396% to $5.9M so operating leverage is starting to kick in.

  • One perennial concern is the high marketing expense for this type of company and while SG&A has declined as a percentage of revenue, this decline slowed down markedly in FY24. S&M declined from 50.12% of revenue in FY23 to 48.49% in FY25 (and G&A actually increased from 33.89% to 34.2% over the same period).
  • Overall adjusted EBITDA Increased: Adjusted EBITDA totaled $9M for the fourth quarter, compared to $5M in the year-ago period.
  • GAAP net loss attributable to common stockholders for Q4 2024 was $883K.
  • WorkSimpli has rebounded and generated more than $1M of adjusted EBITDA each month during the quarter.

  • The company is now generating cash and ended the year with $35M in cash and $9.9M in long-term debt.

Guidance

  • Adjusted EBITDA guidance breaks down $20M from telehealth, $10M to $12M from WorkSimpli (Q4CC): "the increase in EBITDA is essentially growth in the business. There's not a lot of cost structure with that business outside of ad spend and payroll. So most of that is associated with growth." This suggests further operating leverage.
  • Management highlighted investments in areas like their nationwide pharmacy, licensed in 47 states, and expanding their medical and pharmacy benefits infrastructure. They are also building out non-sterile compounding capabilities and expanding their insurance offerings.

Risk

  • While not directly in the first line of fire, the shares will still go down with the rest of the market during a significant downdraft, as we have been witnessing the last couple of trading days.
  • The telehealth market is competitive and there are bigger players with the likes of Amazon (AMZN) and Hims & Hers (HIMS) being better capitalized. However, we think that after being outmaneuvered by HIMS with its cheap GLP-1 compounds, the competitive situation is in part reversing as LifeMD is further along with its payer infrastructure which will be important making brand-name GLP-1 drugs affordable for most patients.
  • The latter has been given something of a setback, as the new US administration refused to broaden Medicare coverage.

Valuation

There is more to come:

So the fully diluted share count is 51.4M, which at $6 per share yields a market cap of $257M and an EV of $232M. Analysts expect FY25 revenue at $268.9M (rising to $319.4M in FY26) which makes the shares very reasonably priced at 0.86x FY25 EV/S.

While analysts still expect a loss (negative EPS of $0.10) this is expected to turn into a profit of EPS $0.19 in FY26. The shares are a lot cheaper than HIMS:

To a large extent that's justified as HIMS is much bigger, grows even faster, and produces lots of cash flow. But we think the end of most compounds and LifeMD's insurance infrastructure might give it some advantages versus HIMS.

Conclusion

  • After a relatively difficult period, the company is back on track with WorkSimpli growing and producing cash again and a re-acceleration in its virtual care platform putting in an impressive 60% growth.
  • Gross margins suffered from the introduction cost of its own pharmacy but will be back to its unusually high 90% or so.
  • Its weight management program was at a disadvantage against HIMS while the latter benefited from cheap compounds, but these are coming to an end and in the meantime, LifeMD has built up an impressive payer infrastructure that benefits members.
  • There are numerous expansions ongoing and emerging for its virtual care platform that will keep growth going for years to come.
  • Our only mild worry is that S&M is declining only very gradually as a percentage of revenue, it's clearly very important for growth.

The upshot: valuation is very reasonable, we see good risk/reward at $5.

data.title: JPMorgan Q1 Preview: Numerous Inflection Points Could Materialize
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase & Co. (NYSE:JPM) is expected to deliver its first-quarter results on Friday, April 11th. The event marks an interesting occasion given a potential juxtaposition between idiosyncratic versus systemic events, whereby JPMorgan's structural benefits could be challenged by broader risks within the banking industry.

According to Seeking Alpha, JPMorgan received ten upward revisions in the build up to Q1, in which the bank is anticipated to reveal $44.05 billion in revenue and a GAAP earnings-per-share of $4.64. By considering the estimate, JPMorgan's ex-ante performance, and the systematic environment, we decided to weigh in on Friday's event.

Previous Outlook

JPMorgan provided a full-year outlook after releasing its fourth-quarter results, where it argued that interest rate sensitivity would see its net income margin recede. On the other end of the spectrum, the bank stated that a decrease in interest rates would leave its balance sheet growth unaffected. To quantify the statement, JPMorgan forecasted firm-wide net interest income of $94 billion for 2025. Whether the prediction plays into Q1 remains to be seen.

As for expenses, JPMorgan said that its cost structure would increase by roughly $4.9 billion in 2025, largely influenced by an inflation-adjusted expense of $2 billion and aggressive investment in technology (among other factors).

The aforementioned is a full-year outlook, yet interpolation can provide a guideline to what's in store for Q1. Additionally, the geopolitical environment has shifted, suggesting events need to be looked at from a holistic sense instead of in isolation.

Our Take

Here's a diagram that illustrates JPMorgan's fiscal 2023 and 2024 results, allowing us to set a base for our outlook. As shown, the bank experienced telling improvements in net interest margin, adjusted overhead percentages, and return-on-equity. Having said that, JPMorgan reflected higher credit costs through an increase in charge-offs.

Outlook To Friday and Beyond

Consumer & Corporate Lending, Ancillary Non-Interest Revenue

The lending environment likely remained robust during Q1. Interest rates, and the U.S.'s in particular, remained within range while mortgage rates stayed pent-up. As such, we believe JPMorgan will table solid net interest income margin on Friday, especially with regards to consumer and mortgage loans. While we argue that a shift in the credit environment has occurred, we think the effect had little influence during Q1.

Looking beyond Q1, we might see the growing macroeconomic instability play a role. We recently published a series of articles, detailing the possibilities of higher credit risk, lower consumer confidence, and more. If realised, our predictions could translate into a less competitive loan pricing environment, higher delinquencies, and wider provisions, especially as much of JPMorgan's retail banking book spans cyclical debt, ranging from mortgages to credit card debt. Moreover, non-interest revenue growth can lower in conjunction with interest income on the basis of lower client conversions and subdued cross-selling.

JPMorgan's corporate client book is much smaller than its consumer and community banking book. However, results can be chunky due to higher implied concentration risk, requiring close assessment during volatile economic periods. Looking back, corporate defaults remained relatively stable with S&P Global (SPG) recording a global rate of 1.91%. In addition, interest rates remained pent-up, likely allowing solid net interest income, assuming funding rates remained intact. In a similar vein to retail credit, we foresee an inflection point in corporate credit; U.S. probability of default rates recently edged up to 9.2%, a figure higher than levels experienced during COVID-19. Higher default figures might not reflect in JPMorgan's Q1 results, though ex-ante performance might be impaired by higher provisions and lower asset valuations.

Investment Banking (Including Markets)

JPMorgan's commercial and investment banking revenue shot up to $17.60 billion during Q4, led by lending and fees. We see a similar trajectory occurring for commercial lending as for corporate and consumer lending, which is, robust Q1 numbers but a challenging outlook.

As for investment banking, we think headwinds transpired in dealmaking during Q1. A comprehensive rebound in global IB materialised during 2024; however, global M&A volumes are down year-to-date with February deal-making settling at merely $68.07 billion, the lowest since Q3 2022. Looking beyond Q1, headwinds might surface though the global trade war, in turn stunning M&A and IPO activity, especially as the war is accompanied by numerous developed markets likely being in late-stage growth cycles.

Market-based revenue ticked up in 2024 amid solid performance in the U.S. equity markets. Unlike 2024, Q1 experienced a rocky road, characterised by a broad-based drawdown in global equity markets. The U.S. stock market experienced telling headwinds in Q1, which seem unlikely to subside for the time being.

Contrary to equities, global bonds have experienced a strong year-to-date, likely due to a pivot into risk-off assets. While we foresee JPMorgan delivering a lacklustre Q1 equity trading report, the bank's fixed income department likely benefitted from government bond repricing and sustained asset-level income. Furthermore, opportunities exist in derivatives. For example, CDS spreads, FX rates, and bond yields have acted abruptly, providing opportunity to banks like JPMorgan to benefit via active return strategies.

In essence, JPMorgan's markets segment will likely report lower trading and intermediary revenues for equity in Q1 and possibly beyond. Nonetheless, we believe fixed income activity could've curbed some of the damage. JPMorgan's markets revenue reached $7.08 billion in Q4, up 21% year-over-year, we think the number might settle slightly lower in the bank's Q1 report.

Risk Metrics

JPMorgan's liquidity coverage ratio remained above 100% in Q4, communicating comprehensive short-term liquidity coverage. Coinciding with that is the bank's average deposit base, which grew by 1.43% quarter-over-quarter. Deposit rates remain competitive, therefore, a drag on liquidity is unlikely to have occurred in Q1.

The bank's standardised common equity tier 1 ratio reached 15.7%, which, in our experience is exceptionally high. Given the brewing market volatility, and the inherent operational risk that coincides with a higher volatility environment, we expect the ratio might deteriorate into the latter stages of 2025. Having said that, it would take a unimaginable event to debase the ratio altogether.

Valuation and Dividend Metrics

A parsimonious view of JPMorgan's valuation metrics illustrates that its price-to-book ratio of 1.72x is above 1x and exceeds its five-year average of 1.59x. Though JPMorgan's unconvincing relative valuation can be combatted by a robust return-on-equity ratio of 18.19%, the aforementioned headwinds likely play into an argument that stock might be priced in.

Ratio Value 5-Y Avg.
Forward P/B 1.72x 1.59x
ROE 18.19% 15.52%
Trailing Dividend Yield 2.35% 2.69%

Source: Seeking Alpha

Concerning its dividend, JPMorgan has a trailing yield of 2.35% and a forward yield of 2.65%. Historical figures convey a low payout ratio combined with sustainable dividend growth. Thus, barring a underwhelming quarter, we might see JPMorgan raise its dividend after releasing its Q1 results.

Final Verdict

A look-back analysis of JPMorgan and market-based variables indicate that the bank could reach its first-quarter guidance on the basis of momentum in the lending environment. However, we believe sluggish year-to-date M&A activity, stock market headwinds, and higher implied credit risk warrants close attention.

Key metrics suggest that JPMorgan has dividend growth prospects, yet its valuation outlook could be clouded by current macro-economic, credit cycle, and political headwinds.

Consensus: 'Hold'

data.title: Curtiss-Wright Corporation: Doing A Lot Of Things Right
data.description:
Over the summer, I believed that shares of Curtiss-Wright Corporation (NYSE:CW) were flying high, perhaps a bit too high. A 50% share price return in just two years time was supported by solid operating growth and a decent chunk of valuation multiple inflation as well.

In the meantime, shares have seen another boom in the fall, to now decline to levels seen last summer, yet great operational improvements have made shares quite a bit cheaper here, as I appreciate the continued operating performance.

Diversified Industrial Operation

Curtiss-Wright is a diversified industrial company with activities split between defense, nuclear and industrial operations. The business has been in operations for nearly a century, having been founded in 1929, and now serves customers in over 100 countries across the globe, generating over $3 billion in sales doing so.

About three-quarter of these sales are generated at home in North America, complemented by activities in Asia-Pacific and Europe. Named after key industrialists in the early part of the 20th century, and having played a key role in World War II, the company has a rich heritage.

Expertise in relevant end markets, long term intimate customer relations, a focus on innovation and a global footprint all help the company win in the marketplace.

Steady As She Goes

Over the past decade, sales have grown some 40% on a cumulative basis to $3.1 billion, which on average works down to 3-4% sales growth. That looks like GDP, or even a GDP+ achievement, yet has been complemented by operating margins having increased a few points to the high teens.

That is not all, as about 20% of the outstanding share base have been bought back, as the combination of all these three items made that earnings nearly tripled to $10 per share. This made that a $70 stock in 2015 broke the $100 mark during the pandemic, the $200 mark in 2023, and even ended up trading at highs of $393 in the fall of last year.

Needless to say, accelerating share price momentum has been fueled by some valuation multiple inflation. 2023 sales, as reported early in 2024, were reported up 11% to $2.85 billion, with adjusted earnings up 15% to $9.38 per share.

The company guided for 2024 sales up around 5% to $2.96-$3.01 billion, with earnings seen up to $10.00-$10.30 per share. After a smaller deal in the first half of last year, the company announced a $200 million deal to acquire Ultra Energy over the summer, adding $65 million in sales, about 2% to all of Curtiss Wright, while pro forma net debt of $900 million was very much in check.

Trading at $272 over the summer, and pegging earnings power at $10.50 per share, I believed that a 25-26 times earnings multiple was demanding in combination with net debt exceeding EBITDA, even as the backlog was up 7% to $3.1 billion, to thereby come in at par with sales. That conclusion was a bit too preliminary, as shares ended up rising further, to highs at $393, pushing up earnings multiples to the high-thirties.

A Boom-Bust

A $270 stock over the summer ended up rising another 40% to highs of $390 in October, only to fall back to the $270 mark here again.

This was aided by solid operating momentum of the business. Alongside the release of the second quarter results in August, the sales guidance was hiked by twenty-five million dollars, with the midpoint of the earnings guidance hiked by more than a quarter to $10.525 per share. The company added more fuel to the fire by announcing an expansion of the buyback program by a hundred million dollars in September.

Through the third quarter, the sales guidance was hiked by another $35-$40 million, with the earnings guidance hiked further to a midpoint of $10.65 per share, while more buybacks were announced towards year-end.

In February, the company reported relatively softer fourth quarter sales, with revenues up 5% to $824 million, as full year sales were up 10% to $3.12 billion. Momentum for the year has been driven by a 12% increase in the Navel & Power business, which generated $1.28 billion in sales, a similar increase in Defense Electronics sales to $910 million and a more modest 5% increase in Aerospace & Industrial sales to $932 million.

GAAP earnings were reported at $10.55 per share, with adjusted earnings reported at $10.90 per share, as most of the discrepancy comes from restructuring costs.

Net debt is down to $664 million which is very modest in relation to $542 million in reported EBIT, let alone EBITDA, despite a quarter of a billion spent on buybacks in 2024, including a big chunk in the fourth quarter.

Looking Forward

The reported results understate the strength of the business, as an order intake of $3.7 billion results in a book-to-bill ratio of 1.2 times, with the backlog up 20% to $3.4 billion, all of which is quite comforting. This sets the company up for a decent 2025 with sales seen up 7-8% to a midpoint of $3.36 billion. Amidst some margin improvements, the company guided for an 11-14% increase in earnings per share $12.10-$12.40 per share, all of which is quite convincing.

Based on this guidance, shares trade at 22-23 times forward earnings, all of which look a lot friendlier than was the case over the summer, amidst the sound operating performance, order intake, although I did not like elevated buybacks at elevated share prices more recently.

All this means that I have grown a bit more appreciative of the business here than I was last summer. I appreciate the strong operating momentum, the solid guidance for 2025, all supported by stronger order intake and growing backlog.

These very strong achievements are to be applauded, with the business greatly positioned between defense, aerospace and nuclear. With more room for sales growth and more orders reported early in 2025, the picture looked solid ahead of the tariff carnage here.

While appeal has improved a great deal, many parts of the markets have sold off greatly. Despite the more than $100 pullback since the highs in the fall, this means that shares are still an outperformer if I compare the current share price to last summer.

All this means that while I have grown more appreciative of the business, I am not just willing to dip my toes into the water here.

data.title: Capital Power: Generating Low Risk 15% Yields With Covered Calls
data.description:
Note: All amounts referenced are in Canadian Dollars. The stock prices referenced are the ones trading on the TSX. No reference is made to the OTC tickers.

On our previous coverage of Capital Power (TSX:CPX:CA), we suggested that the stock was well ahead of its fundamentals and likely to move lower. We also reminded investors that Capital Power Corporation CUM RESET PFD1 (TSX:CPX.PR.A:CA) shares were a better alternative. The key comment on the common shares was this.

At present, we think that is a fair estimate as well, and we think the shares are worth around $42-$46 per share.

Source: Preferreds Look Like The Best Bet

We were definitely early in the bearish call on the common, but the preferred shares responded right away. Eventually, as the AI bubble came to its rightful full implosion, even the power producers responded and CPX:CA turned tail. The preferreds have outperformed the common shares by about 24% since then.

We update our views today and tell you why you should be looking at the common shares for income, but not for the reasons you might suspect.

Recent Results & Guidance

Q4-2024 results were weaker than consensus with adjusted EBITDA coming in at $330 million. Estimates were in the $355 million range. Adjusted funds from operations (AFF)) missed by a smaller margin but were still about 4% under estimates. CPX:CA has disappointed through most of 2024 as estimates have been far above what the company could manage. The reason for the run-up though was that it got caught in the "data-center" hype. You know the story that went around that we will have acres and acres of these data centers to tell us exactly how many "rs" there are in Strawberry. We never bought it but the market did and that led to disappointment. Based on the company commentary and analyst estimates, we think it will deliver about 5% in EBITDA growth and a similar amount in AFFO growth per share for 2025.

The growth is going to be powered by the new assets and partially offset by collapsing Alberta power prices.

To be fair, the company never got caught up in the hype. Their guidance has always been steady and they never overplayed the buzz words.

But as the stock price took off, analysts became the tail that tried to wag the dog. Hey the stock is going up, so let's raise our price targets. You can see that the average stock price target from analysts moved up 50% from April 2024 to April 2025.

Guess how much the EBITDA estimates moved in this timeframe? Negative 0.24%.

The last time we saw such comedy was for TELUS Corporation (TU), where the company kept talking about growth as estimates kept trending lower. Analysts bought that silly story as well leading some spectacularly bad returns for investors that dived in at the highs. But all that being said and done, the stock is now actually lower. And that is where the magic happens.

Why This Could Be An Opportunity

CPX:CA is well setup for 2025. The 6% dividend yield consumes a very small portion of the cash flow.

More importantly, the company has aggressive hedges in place for 2025 and 2026 power prices. We have also found that both discounts to NAV and price to sales ratios work far better to identify buy-points for CPX:CA than price to AFFO ratios. On both metrics CPX:CA is look at least moderately attractive. On a price to NAV (which is calculated via discounted cash flows of its facilities), CPX:CA is trading at one of the largest discounts, outside of COVID-19.

The NAV is to some extent a function of demand for the power plants that CPX:CA owns and that in turn has been driven higher by this data center rush. We remain skeptical overall on the idea that AI will increase computing demands materially. Nonetheless, such a wide discount does increase your odds of making money.

The price to sales ratio offers investors a chance to adjust for the fact that margins are not a birthright. They go up and down and the only way to make money in the long run is to be cognizant of this. 1.52X while not a screaming buy, is definitely in the right postal code.

But the deal clincher is neither of these two. If the quest is to deliver risk-adjusted returns than one has to take advantage of the volatility and here the options are priced just beautifully. We love longer-dated calls and in our opinion that is the only real way to generate a real risk-adjusted yield. While option funds keep playing with weekly options, we find them to be horrid choices for the common investors. Here, we will look at the March 2026 calls.

The premiums are excellent as the volatility is very high for the general market and also for this stock. So this gives us a 15.73% annualized yield. The real kicker is that you get this yield even if the stock drops about 10%.

We think this is a great setup and one prudent investors can consider if they have a mentality of getting high yield with low risk.

Preferred Shares

With the appreciation of CPX.PR.A:CA and the drop in price of CPX:CA, we don't see the former as compelling any more. We think the reset which is about 8 months away, is likely not worth hanging around for. CPX:CA covered calls beat CPX.PR.A:CA's buy and hold in our books. And it is not even close. We would stay out of the preferred shares.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Approaching Nvidia In Stoic Calm: Fourth Valuation Update
data.description:
As someone interested in technology but whose area of expertise does not lie in a related field, I initiated my Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) coverage in June 2024 by soberly focusing on observable financial data and implied market expectations. Sticking to our area of expertise, this is what most of us financial analysts can sensibly do. With this approach, I refrained from judging Wall Street analysts' estimates. Instead, the idea was to do nothing more than derive what markets are pricing into Nvidia.

As a result, it appeared that the “Best Case” was priced into the stock, as I had also titled the first article. Following research pieces of mine kept calling for the “Double-Digit” range as opening potentially fair buying opportunities into Nvidia.

Now, with markets tanking, we have finally reached double-digits. Let us therefore not turn into panic mode and assess market expectations for Nvidia and its valuation considerations equally soberly to find out whether buying at this double-digit price already appears rational in the current environment.

The Beauty Of Valuation

Valuation is and always has been important. When valuation is thrown overboard, exuberance emerges, and that is what has partly happened in recent years.

Applied discount rates have a significant impact on valuation results and should not be based on gut feeling. Although for subjective valuation every investor is free to choose a discount rate that suits him or her, I find it easier to approximate an objectively observable company value, by deriving fair discount rates based on current alternatives and inherent risks that are statistically quantifiable.

Therefore, I continue my cost of capital calculation from last year. See below, how parameters have changed since November 2024. The two most notable changes occurred in (1) the equity risk premium and in (2) Nvidia’s company beta:

  1. Having landed at historically low levels beforehand, equity risk premia have finally increased again, meaning that investors are now more aware of the uncertainties ahead and want to see them priced into current prices, aka want to see them discounted.
  2. Nvidia’s raw beta factors have increased significantly to 2.46 over a 24 months regression period and 1.96 over a 60 months regression period, resulting in a Blume adjusted average of 1.81. This means a significantly elevated beta factor.

All together brings my WACC up by one percentage point to 13%.

Due to Nvidia’s unchanged high Return on Assets and reinvestment behavior, the perpetual growth rate base assumption of 5% remains unchanged.

Proceeding with EPS and cashflow projections, the consensus estimates have shifted upwards only slightly by roughly single digit percentages since November.

Net cash has increased, also slightly bolstering value, yet almost negligible.

Summing up, the significantly increased discount rate mitigates all aforementioned positives. Altogether, the model lands at a fair value range that remains almost completely unchanged between 60 and 70 USD per share. The range is based on worst to best analysts’ estimates.

However, let us apply sensitivities to highly important input parameters such as WACC and terminal growth rate. This shows that at $98, we still stand above the strong green inner range of reasonable valuations, but we approached this area significantly when we saw prices below $90 during Monday’s market opening. In fact, this brought the share price further into the light green widespread sensitivity ranges than it has been since I started covering Nvidia. Nevertheless, do not confuse this with an assessment of a cheap valuation, which is still not evident.

What Everyone Is Talking About – And Some Less Obvious Aspects

There are some talking points that are currently determining the discussion around Nvidia. This chapter aims at briefly addressing them without diving too deep. Because what the completely neutral valuation approach based on publicly available data beforehand did was fusing all these risks and potentially alternating outcomes together and pour it into one number. That was "The Beauty Of Valuation." And these are some narratives behind the numbers:

Concerns surrounding AI-chip overcapacity. Some reports around Microsoft cancelling data center leases fuel these theses. However, an aggregate of reported CAPEX from hyper scalers so far does not indicate a significant slowdown in datacenter investments. Yet, available reports only cover periods including December 2024. In my last coverage, TTM CAPEX of Alphabet, Meta, Microsoft, Amazon and Tesla totaled $210 billion. Nvidia’s TTM revenue stood at $113 billion. Since then, the aggregate TTM CAPEX of those very same companies have increased by another 12% quarter over quarter, versus Nvidia’s TTM revenue up 15% QoQ.

This seamlessly brings us to revenue recognition and cashflow. As examined in November, Nvidia’s Receivables have recently increased faster than Revenue QoQ. However, Days Sales Outstanding (DSO) are still within historically normal levels, yet show an uptick as well. Free Cashflow, as another indicator, continues to back revenue but also started to grow slower than revenue in the most recent quarter.

The potential effects of tariffs and the ongoing trade disputes in general are thoroughly examined in other articles, and are ideally also implicitly baked into the input parameters of my earlier valuation.

Lastly, Nvidia has been facing an expectations problem for quite some time. Significantly exceeded expectations lead analysts to continuously revise their estimates upwards to a point where we began to see surprises decrease meaningfully. It is not impossible that at some point the increased analysts’ expectations become too hard to beat and will instead be missed.

Takeaway And Personal Involvement Disclaimer

I accumulated Nvidia at two occasions last year with an average share price cost basis between $100 and $110, bringing my position in the red by less than ten percentage points so far. Furthermore, I kept Nvidia’s portfolio weight at a low single-digit percentage. Given the results of this valuation update, I am not willing to double down just yet. Shall we see the base case valuation range in the mid-60s without fundamental deterioration of Nvidia’s business, that would be the next range for larger scale accumulation. Otherwise, I would remain highly content with the currently small position size and enjoy gains on this. Instead, I continue to relatively overweigh the hyper scalers themselves, as they can quickly decide to stop "hyper scaling," leaving cash for themselves and their shareholders instead of paying it to chip manufacturers and designers.

data.title: Rivian: Tesla's Loss Is This Automaker's Gain
data.description:
Sharp fear has overtaken the markets, suddenly sending the major U.S. indices into a correction after a roaring years-long rally. And yet underneath the volatility, investors who are able to stomach some short-term pain have a great opportunity to pick up shares of fantastic growth stocks at a major discount.

Though it's difficult to see this amid the haze of pessimism, a lot of things are going right for Rivian (NASDAQ:RIVN), the second-largest EV manufacturer in the U.S. Its main rival, Tesla (TSLA), is facing unprecedented global backlash as a means of protest over Elon Musk's rapid downsizing of the federal government. Meanwhile, new U.S. tariffs are expected to sharply raise the prices of foreign cars, making Rivian's typically higher-end models look more balanced on the pricing spectrum.

Year to date, shares of Rivian have lost ~15% of their value, outpacing losses in the S&P 500. Rivian has held on to its value a bit better than Tesla has this year, of course, but in my view, Rivian has a much better chance of a nearer-term rebound.

I last wrote a bullish note on Rivian in December, when the stock was trading near $14 per share. Since then, Rivian has fallen in sympathy with the rest of the stock market: but in my view, it has become a relatively much better bargain, especially with strong production expected in FY25 and deliveries ahead of expectations so far in Q1. With this in mind, I'm reiterating my buy rating on this company.

Stronger-than-expected deliveries presents a contrast to Tesla

To me, one of the core reasons that Rivian is such an appealing buy now is that its deliveries have been reasonably strong, while Tesla has fallen victim to global protests over its brand - not just in the U.S., but across the Atlantic in Europe as well, especially in Germany where Musk has had very public feuds with the German government.

The chart above showcases Tesla's production and deliveries for Q1. The company's 336.7k delivery units, reported in early April, fell roughly -11% below consensus estimates for 377k deliveries, while also declining -13% y/y versus 387k deliveries in the year-ago quarter. This is an indication of the growing backlash against Elon Musk that is cementing itself in today's zeitgeist, and is a troubling look for the world's largest EV maker ahead of its planned launch of the next-gen, lower-cost Model 3 and Model Y variants.

The situation for Tesla is so dim that Elon Musk held a widely-publicized all hands meeting with Tesla employees in an attempt to convince them to stay with the company and hold on to their shares, talking up future innovations such as the Optimus robot. But no matter how much spin is put on the situation, there is little denial that Tesla's sales momentum has slowed dramatically.

Rivian isn't exactly growing like a weed, especially after a planned factory shutdown this year (to convert lines ahead of its planned R2 model launch) and a shortage of a critical component. But even though Rivian's deliveries are in a y/y decline (which is to be expected ahead of a key new product launch, as buyers wait for the new model to come out), results are better than expected. Also in April on the exact same day that Tesla reported its Q1 production and delivery metrics, Rivian reported 8,640 cars delivered - which came in 8% higher than the "approximately 8,000" vehicles that the company had originally guided to. Rivian also confirmed its FY25 delivery target of 46-51k vehicles, the high end of which would represent roughly flat y/y performance versus 51.5k vehicles delivered in FY24 - again, not a bad result ahead of the R2 launch in FY26.

Relative to other companies, an escalating trade war may have less impact on Rivian

First off, no company in the world is immune to escalating trade wars. Some companies (like retailers that manufacture in China) will have direct, first-order impacts, while other companies will feel only a second-order sting from a slowing macroeconomy.

Rivian, in my view, falls somewhere in the middle of that spectrum: but relative to many other companies, Rivian has the potential to fare better amid escalating tariffs. For one, Rivian manufactures its vehicles primarily in the U.S. Importantly, U.S. manufacturing does not mean that Rivian's business is immune from tariffs, as the company surely sources at least some of its parts internationally (the company notes that its FY25 outlook incorporates tariff risks known at the time, but does not break out specific impacts).

However, given that one of the focuses of Trump's tariff plan is to reinvigorate U.S. manufacturing, I think that Rivian's $6.6 billion Biden-era loan from the Department of Energy, which was finalized in early January and will be disbursed over a multi-year period, is likely safe from being rescinded or downsized by the new administration - especially since the proceeds of the loan are intended to boost Rivian's R2 manufacturing capacity in Illinois. The company notes that the deal will help bring in 7,500 new manufacturing jobs.

Furthermore, Rivian is (at least for now) also relatively shielded from the impacts of retaliatory tariffs from a top-line perspective. Rivian does not break out its exact sales by region, but currently the company sells only into the U.S. with a minor portion of sales in Canada.

Tesla, however, has massive international exposure. The U.S. was just shy of half of the company's FY24 revenue, with China representing 21% of FY24 sales and the rest of the world (primarily Europe) representing another 30%.

China has already instituted retaliatory tariffs against the U.S. The European Commission, which has to coordinate among its member states to decide on a response, is preparing similar moves. In both cases, sharper import duties will contribute to an already-weak sales environment and negative brand backlash in these key markets.

Margin progress

Lastly, one of the most important long-term indicators that has me enthusiastic on Rivian is its continued progress in gross margins. In Q4, Rivian hit a 10% gross margin, scoring above breakeven for the first time in the company's history, as manufacturing scales have matured, and the company enjoys a proportionally larger mix of revenue from software and services.

Management notes that it has removed an incredible $31,000 of COGS out of each vehicle unit sold in Q4, relative to the prior-year Q4. The company also notes that it is 95% complete with finalizing the bill of materials for the upcoming R2 (with mass production slated to begin in mid-2026), with a BOM that is roughly half of the current R1 models (giving the company plenty of room to slash prices relative to the current R1S and R1T models and finally push Rivian into the mass market). Per CEO and founder RJ Scaringe's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Now, of course, the things we see in terms of development of our product and the build out of the expansion to our plant, those are very visible to someone driving by a normal or to see our test vehicles out on the road. But we've also sourced 95% of the bill of materials. And the bill of materials in R2 is expected to be roughly half of what we have in R1. And the other non-bill of material COGS targets are significantly less than what we have in R1, actually significantly less than half of what we have in R1. And so, the overall cost structure that we're planning for an R2 is a night and day improvement relative to what we launched with and what we have today on our R1."

Needless to say, we'll have to see how tariffs imposed on internationally sourced parts will play a role into Rivian's BOM development. Still, we note the continuous improvement in the company's cost profile and the heavy cost takeouts for next year's model give Rivian a great baseline to work with.

Key takeaways

As always, in hyper-volatile situations, investors should avoid giving into the surrounding negative emotions and focus on long-term drivers. Rivian, in my view, is a perfect buy-the-dip candidate because it may benefit from tariffs charged on foreign automakers (plus cultural backlash against Tesla), while its primarily domestic sales base shields it from foreign retaliation. At the same time, the company continues to make progress toward achieving richer gross margins, while marching ahead with its lower-cost R2 models that are expected to hit the road in 2026.

Stay long here and use the drop as a buying opportunity.
